revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:إيتاكو]][[de:Itako (Schamanismus)]][[en:Itako]][[es:Itako]][[fr:Itako]][[id:Itako]][[it:Itako (sciamane)]][[ja:イタコ]][[ko:이타코]][[sv:Itako]][[th:อิตาโกะ]][[zh:潮來 (巫女)]]


{{short description|Spirit mediums of Japan, strictly blind women}}
{{About|the profession|the city in {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}|Itako, Ibaraki}}
{{italic title}}
[[File:Inako 2006-10-09.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An ''itako'' at the autumn Inako Taisai festival at {{ill|Mount Osore|en|Mount Osore|qid=Q1377333|lt=Mount Osore|1=Mount Osore}}, Aomori Prefecture, Japan.]]

{{nihongo||イタコ|'''Itako'''|lead=yes}}, also known as {{nihongo||市子|ichiko}} or {{nihongo||オガミサマ|ogamisama}}, are blind women who train to become {{ill|Mediumship|en|Mediumship|qid=Q25416928|lt=spiritual mediums|1=Mediumship}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref name="Fackler-NYTimes" /> Training involves severe [[:en:Ascetic|Ascetic]] practices, after which the woman is said to be able to communicate with Japanese Shinto spirits, ''[[kami]]'', and the spirits of the dead.<ref name="Blacker" /> ''Itako'' perform rituals tied to communication with the dead<ref name="Blacker" /> and {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|qid=Q1043197|lt=Divination|1=Divination}}.<ref name="Ivy" /> The practice has been on the decline, with only 20 living ''itako'' in Japan, all more than 40 years old.<ref name="Fackler-NYTimes" />

Training for ''itako'' traditionally began at a very young age, and included ritualized exposure to cold water. Hundreds of buckets of ice water could be poured on their bodies over the course of a few days.<ref name="Blacker" /> This education for ''itako'' takes about three years, and also includes memorization of songs and {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}}s.<ref name="Kawamura" /> At the end of this training, a ceremony is held, announcing the marriage of the ''itako'' and her patron spirit.<ref name="UNESCO" />

Scholars suggest that blindness has long been associated with spiritual powers in Japan.<ref name="deFerranti" />{{rp|24}} Furthermore, options for those with severe vision impairment to become self-sufficient were limited in ancient times.<ref name="Breen2013">{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|date=2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978dd36827044}}</ref>{{rp|190}} This led many families to send young blind women to ''itako'' apprenticeships<ref name="Breen2013"/>{{rp|190}} up until the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, which outlawed ''itako'' rituals outright.<ref name="Josephson">{{cite book|last1=Josephson|first1=Jason Ānanda|title=The invention of religion in Japan|date=2012|publisher=The University of Chicago Press|location=Chicago|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref>{{rp|179}}

Today, ''itako'' are most commonly associated with {{ill|Mount Osore|en|Mount Osore|qid=Q1377333|lt=Mt. Osore|1=Mount Osore}} in Aomori prefecture. There, ''itako'' gather for an annual festival to channel the dead for thousands of tourists.<ref name="Schatt2003" />{{rp|31}} ''Itako'' perform ceremonies to communicate with spirits of the recently deceased,<ref name="Blacker">{{cite book|last1=Blacker|first1=Carmen|editor1-last=Earhart|editor1-first=Byron|title=Religion in the Japanese experience : sources and interpretations|date=1997|publisher=Wadsworth Pub. Co.|location=Belmont, CA|isbn=0534524613|pages=130–135|edition=2nd}}</ref>  including those of aborted and stillborn children.<ref name="Breen2013" />{{rp|198}}

== History ==
''Itako'' are always blind, or have very poor vision.<ref name="Horii" />{{rp|279}} In pre-modern Japanese society, blindness was widely associated with spiritual capabilities; after the introduction of Buddhism, it was considered evidence of a karmic debt.<ref name="deFerranti" />{{rp|24}} These beliefs lent an aura of "ambiguous sacred status"<ref name="deFerranti" />{{rp|24}} to the blind. 
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo era|1=Edo period}}, women were expected to contribute to family wages.<ref name="Kawamura" /> However, blind women of the era had limited opportunities to support themselves or their families.<ref name="Breen2013"/>{{rp|190}} The reputation of blindness and spiritual abilities led many families to seek training for young girls, typically aged dd to 13, in a folk religious tradition in which the young girl was wed to a deity, and there on able to communicate with spirits.<ref name="Breen2013" />{{rp|190}} Despite this power, blind women who became ''itako'' were still considered to occupy one of the lowest social strata within the community, especially those who relied on community support for financing their training.<ref name="Kawamura" />

''Itako'' are thought to have risen from an ascetic cult of the Edo era, the ''[[yamabushi]]'', male monks who were encountered during popular pilgrimages to the Kumano mountains in the 9th–10th centuries.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|2}} These monks had wives who traveled with them, selling amulets and channeling the dead through trances. Meanwhile, women in the north performed the dances and rituals of shrine ''[[miko]]''.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|2}} Over time, the two groups merged, creating the modern concept of ''itako''.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|2}}

In 1873, the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|qid=Q122804|lt=Meiji era government|1=Meiji Constitution}} attempted to ban ''itako'' and their associated ''kuchiyose'' rituals as a means of encouraging the adoption of modernized medicine.<ref name="Josephson"/>{{rp|179}} An estimated 200 ''itako'' were practicing at the time.<ref name="Goozner" /> The law led to the arrest of mediums across Japan; by 1875, ''itako'' and their healing rituals were specifically targeted and could be arrested on sight.<ref name="Josephson" />{{rp|181}} Arrests of ''itako'' were based on charges such as spreading superstition, to obstruction of medical practices.<ref name="Ikegami">{{cite web|last1=Ikegami|first1=Yoshimasa|title=Local Newspaper Coverage of Folk Shamans in Aomori PrefectureI|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/cpjr/folkbeliefs/ikegami.html|website=Kokugakuin University|access-date=12 March 2016}}</ref> Newspaper accounts of these arrests indicate that ''itako'' were commonly attributing illnesses to possession of the ill by cats or foxes.<ref name="Ikegami" /> Newspaper reports at the time tended to refer to ''itako'' in negative terms, and often associated the arrest of ''itako'' and the arrest of prostitutes. One editorial wrote, "These miko disgorge reckless and empty gossip, squeezing the pockets of the common people, and prostitutes tempt loose men and rob them of their money. While the professions are different, they are the same in disrupting public morals and deceiving people of their possessions."<ref name="Ikegami" />

Public support, however, continued. Shortly before, and after, the surrender of Japan in 1945, many families sought out ''itako'' to communicate with the war dead, particularly those who were lost in combat abroad. Just before the end of the war, ''itako'' were also called upon to conduct "living seances" (''ikiguchi'') with soldiers overseas.<ref name="Ikegami" /> An editorial by intellectual Orikuchi Shinobu at the time noted that the ''itako'' in Aomori prefecture had "not been subject to prohibition", in contrast to ''itako'' and shaman in other prefectures, and that "persecution would be counterproductive".<ref name="Ikegami" /> News stories reported locals occasionally interfering with the police attempts to arrest prominent ''itako''.<ref name="Ikegami" />

Today, the small number of living ''itako'' gather annually during the {{ill|Obon|en|Obon|qid=Q1154888|lt=Obon|1=Obon}} holiday at {{ill|Mount Osore|en|Mount Osore|qid=Q1377333|lt=Mt. Osore|1=Mount Osore}} in Aomori, known locally as "Mount Dread". The gathering has received televised news coverage since the 1960s.<ref name="Schatt2003">{{cite book|last1=Schattschneider|first1=Ellen|title=Immortal wishes : labor and transcendence on a Japanese sacred mountain|date=2003|publisher=Duke University press|location=Durham (N. C.)|isbn=9780822330622}}</ref>{{rp|30}}

== Description ==
''Itako'' typically carry several artifacts. These include a kind of box called a {{nihongo||外法箱|{{ill|Gehōbako|en|Gehōbako|qid=Q123399072|lt=Gehōbako|1=Gehōbako}}|extra=literally "non-Buddhist box", in reference to practices outside of Buddhism}}, which contains secret items that may be representative of a protective spirit, or ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="Schiffer" />{{rp|180}}<ref name="Fairchild1962" />{{rp|66}} Blacker describes an old folk tale tying the box, and its contents, to an ''inugami'' ritual.<ref name="Blacker 2013">{{cite book|last1=Blacker|first1=Carmen|title=Collected Writings of Modern Western Scholars on Japan|date=2013|publisher=Routledge}}</ref>{{rp|181}} In this ritual, a dog is buried up to its neck and starved, while staring at food too far for it to reach. The ''itako'' place the animal's skull into a box and offer its spirit a daily offering of food. In return, the spirit enters homes of her patrons and provides detailed information about the dead.<ref name="Blacker 2013" />{{rp|181}}

''Itako'' also carry a black cylinder, often {{ill|Bamboo|en|Bamboo|qid=Q670887|lt=Bamboo|1=Bamboo}}, containing another protective charm and their certificate of ''itako'' training.<ref name="Schiffer" />{{rp|180}}<ref name="Fairchild1962" />{{rp|66}} The cylinders are said to be used to trap the spirits of animals that attempt to possess a human being.<ref name="Fairchild1962" />{{rp|66}} Finally, the women carry {{nihongo|beaded necklaces|いらたか念珠|irataka nenju}}, which are used in ceremonies and made up of beads and animal bones.<ref name="Schiffer" />{{rp|180}}<ref name="Fairchild1962" />{{rp|66}} The bones are typically jaw bones of deer or foxes, but have also included bear teeth, eagle claws, or shells.<ref name="Fairchild1962" />{{rp|66}}

==Definition==
The term "itako" has associations with beggars, and some mediums reject the use of the term.<ref name="Dirks">{{cite book|title=In near ruins : cultural theory at the end of the century|date=1998|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|isbn=9780816631230|editor-last=Dirks|location=Minneapolis|chapter=5|last1=Ivy|first1=Marilyn|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/innearruinscultu0000unse}}</ref>{{rp|dd6}} One theory suggests the term derived from "eta no ko", or "child of the ''eta''", referring to the Japanese {{ill|Burakumin|en|Burakumin|qid=Q548787|lt=Burakumin|1=Burakumin}} social class who were once associated with death.<ref name="Dirks" />{{rp|dd6}} Other possible derivations include the use of wood pieces, ''ita'', for the ceremonial writing of the name of the deceased.<ref name="Dirks" />{{rp|dd6}}

The ''itako'' (then ''ita'') are first referenced in poem #1773 of the ancient {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} poem collection, the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}''.<ref name="Naumann">{{cite journal|last1=Naumann|first1=Nelly|title=The itako of North-Eastern Japan and Their Chants|journal=Nachrichten der Gesellschaft für Natur- und Völkerkunde Ostasiens|date=1992|volume=152|url=https://www2.uni-hamburg.de/oag/noag/noag1992_4.pdf|access-date=9 March 2016|language=de|archive-date=7 August 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150807133445/http://www.uni-hamburg.de/oag/noag/noag1992_4.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref>{{rp|2}} Anthropologist Wilhem Schiffer describes a local legend about the practice of recruiting blind women into {{ill|Shamanism|en|Shamanism|qid=Q42042|lt=Shamanism|1=Shamanism}}. According to this legend, the practice began in an undetermined era when blind children were killed every 5 years. A local official, impressed with a blind woman's ability to describe her environment despite her lack of vision, determined that the blind must have special powers. Rather than being killed, he pressed for the blind to study {{ill|Necromancy|en|Necromancy|qid=Q337935|lt=Necromancy|1=Necromancy}}.<ref name="Schiffer" />{{rp|182}}

''Itako'' is the common term for these women in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori|1=Aomori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|qid=Q48326|lt=Iwate|1=Iwate Prefecture}} and {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|qid=Q81863|lt=Akita|1=Akita Prefecture}} prefectures, but the term can vary by location. They are also commonly called ''ogamisama'' in southern Iwate and {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|qid=Q47896|lt=Miyagi|1=Miyagi Prefecture}} prefectures; ''miko, mogodo, onakama'' or ''waka'' in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}; and ''waka'' in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|qid=Q71707|lt=Fukushima|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|qid=Q44843|lt=Tochigi|1=Tochigi Prefecture}} and {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}.<ref name="Kawamura" />

===''Kamisama''===
There are similarities to another group of shaman women, the ''kamisama''. Both ''kamisama'' and ''itako'' believe in a marriage to a spirit, and both follow the Buddhist deity [[Acala|Fudo Myoo]].<ref name="Schatt2003" />{{rp|32}} However, kamisama are sighted, typically claiming prophetic powers in the aftermath of a traumatic disease.<ref name="Schatt2003" />{{rp|31}} Unlike ''itako'', they are associated with small Shinto shrines, which they may operate themselves. Kamisama tend to view ''itako'' with suspicion, though ethnographers have found that kamisama often associate themselves with ''itako'' and ''itako'' traditions.<ref name="Schatt2003" />{{rp|31}}

==Rituals==
[[File:Osorezan Aomori.JPG|thumb|Osorezan and Lake Usori. Aomori prefecture, Japan.]]
''Itako'' are said to have the ability to communicate with both kami and the dead.<ref name="Blacker"/> An ''itako'''s role varies depending on the spirit she is connected to.<ref name="Blacker" /> Ceremonies vary by prefecture, but typically ''itako'' are called upon to communicate with kami spirits to garner favor or advisement on harvests, or to communicate with the spirits of the dead, particularly the recently deceased.<ref name="Blacker" /> The rituals typically take place on {{ill|Mount Osore|en|Mount Osore|qid=Q1377333|lt=Mount Osore|1=Mount Osore}}, which is locally associated with this "traditional" ritual, though evidence suggests ''itako'' withdrew to the mountain to escape pressure from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji state|1=Meiji era}}.<ref name="Ivy">{{cite book|last1=Ivy|first1=Marilyn|title=Discourses of the vanishing modernity, phantasm, Japan|date=1995|publisher=University of Chicago Press|location=Chicago|isbn=9780226388342}}</ref>{{rp|174}} The ceremony had traditionally taken place in the ancestral home of the dead.<ref name="Blacker" />

Ceremonies draw on a series of Shinto practices, but most often call upon Buddhist gods.<ref name="Goozner">{{cite news|last1=Goozner|first1=Merrill|title=Japan's Blind Women Seers Becoming An Endangered Species|url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/1992/12/18/japans-blind-women-seers-becoming-an-endangered-species/|access-date=12 March 2016|publisher=Chicago Tribune|date=18 December 1992}}</ref> Some chants of the itako are similar to secret practices of [[:en:Yoshida Shinto|Yoshida Shinto]], and may be tied to practices at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise shrine]].<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|8}}

Itako perform the ceremony using {{ill|Tsugaru dialect|en|Tsugaru dialect|qid=Q908025|lt=Tsugaru dialect|1=Tsugaru dialect}}, and a series of "[[taboo]] words"<ref name="Ivy" />{{rp|186}} that are not understood by all native Japanese speakers. Locals, who understand the Tsugaru dialect, are therefore often employed as translators for the ''itako''.<ref name="Ivy" />{{rp|186}} Folklorists suggest that this allows the ''itako'' to avoid any need to personalize the communication from the departed spirit.<ref name="Ivy" />{{rp|186}}

===''Kuchiyose''===
The ritual of contact with the dead, {{Nihongo||口寄せ|kuchiyose}}, has been named an {{ill|Intangible cultural heritage|en|Intangible cultural heritage|qid=Q84036549|lt=intangible cultural heritage asset of Japan|1=Intangible cultural heritage}}.<ref name="McClatchy">{{cite news|title=Benefits of shaman on patients studied|url=https://www.highbeam.com/doc/1G1-149062964.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/2018dd19051609/https://www.highbeam.com/doc/1G1-149062964.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 November 2018|access-date=|agency=McClatchy-Tribune Information Services|publisher=Yomiuri Shimbun|url-access=|date=4 August 2006}}</ref> The ritual has been documented as early as 1024 AD in the {{Nihongo||栄花物語|[[:en:Eiga monogatari|Eiga monogatari]]}}.<ref name="Horii">{{cite journal|last1=Horii|first1=Ichiro|title=Shamanism in Japan|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|date=December 1975|volume=2|issue=4|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2386}}</ref>{{rp|280}}

The ritual is held during a funeral or anniversary of a death,<ref name="UNESCO" /> though some ''itako'' claim the dead cannot be contacted until 100 days have passed.<ref name="Dirks" />{{rp|105}} Once the spirit communicates its own status in the afterlife, patrons receive advice and predictions for the future.<ref name="Ivy" />

Visitors to the ''itako'' typically bring fruit, candy or other gifts, and offer the age, relationship, and gender of the deceased, but not the name.<ref name="Dirks" />{{rp|dd6}}

During the ceremony, purifying rice and salt are scattered, and a spirit is said to enter the body of the ''itako''. Gods are called forward and asked to compel the desired spirit or ghost to come forward.<ref name="UNESCO" /> Calling the dead usually involves calling upon a hierarchy of spirits in reverse order, beginning with kami and rising to the level of ghosts.<ref name="Blacker" />   Then, the local kami is called forward to protect those attending the ceremony. During the summoning of the deceased (''hotoke oroshi''), the itako sings songs, called ''kudoki'', said to be relayed by the contacted spirit.<ref name="UNESCO" /><ref name="Miura">{{cite book|last1=Miura|first1=Teeji|title=Asian Musics in an Asian Perspective: Report of Asian Traditional Performing Arts|date=1977|publisher=Heibonsha|location=Tokyo, Japan|pages=83–85}}</ref> The spirit of the dead arrives and shares memories of its life and the afterlife, answering questions for patrons.<ref name="Dirks" />{{rp|105}} Then, the spirits are sent away, and songs are sung about "hell, insects, and birds."<ref name="UNESCO" /><ref name="Miura" /> A final spell is repeated three times: “The old fox in the Shinoda woods, when he cries during the day, then he does not cry in the night”.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|12}} The interaction lasts about 15 minutes.<ref name="Dirks" />{{rp|105}}

As recently as 1962, ethnographers observing ''itako'' rituals noted a shaking of hands and use of special voices when channeling the dead.<ref name="Fairchild1962">{{cite journal|last1=Fairchild|first1=William P.|title=Shamanism in Japan (1–122)|journal=Asian Ethnology|date=1962|volume=21|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/457|access-date=2016-03-09|archive-date=2016-03-09|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160309134301/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/457|url-status=dead}}</ref>{{rp|62}} More recent observers note that ''itako'' sang in their own voices, without any visual performance of entering a trance-state. The ritual songs are typically repeated to many patrons, suggesting that the ''itako'' are understood, even by their patrons, to be theatrical performers.<ref name="Ivy" /> {{rp|176}} Nonetheless, a survey of 670 people with chronic illnesses in the area around Osorezan Mountain, home of many ''itako'', showed that 35% of those patients had visited ''itako'' to take part in a ''kuchiyose'' ritual.<ref name="McClatchy" />

===''Mizuko kuyō''===
{{nihongo||水子供養|{{ill|Mizuko kuyō|en|Mizuko kuyō|qid=Q2879429|lt=Mizuko kuyō|1=Mizuko kuyō}}}} is a ceremony performed for mothers who have lost their children in childbirth or through abortions.<ref name="Breen2013" />{{rp|198}} The ritual, Buddhist in nature, gives the unborn or stillborn child a name, and then calls upon the protection of the spirit Jizo. The ceremony is considered by many to be a scam preying on grieving mothers, owing to its relatively recent origins in the 1960s. Others, however, see the practice as addressing a spiritual need created by Japan's legalization of abortion in 1948.<ref name="NYTimesSteinfels">{{cite news|last1=Steinfels|first1=Peter|title=Beliefs|url=https://www.nytimes.com/1992/08/15/us/beliefs-891892.html|access-date=12 March 2016|work=New York Times|date=15 August 1992}}</ref>

===New Year Ebisu===
''Itako'' must learn a chant known as the {{nihongo|New Year Ebisu|正月えべす|shōgatsu-ebesu}}, a celebration of the New Year delivered in travels at the start of Spring.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|5}} The chant mixes Buddhist references (such as peacocks) and aspects of the Japanese national anthem. Scholars suggest the language reveals the chant is derived from songs associated with the ''{{ill|Hinin|en|Hinin|qid=Q3135765|lt=Hinin|1=Hinin}}''.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|5}}

===''Kamioroshi''===
The {{Nihongo||神降ろし|kamioroshi}} ritual is a musical performance dedicated to {{ill|Oshira-sama|en|Oshira-sama|qid=Q11071206|lt=Oshirasama|1=Oshira-sama}}, a god of family.<ref name="UNESCO" /> This ritual includes the performance of a song describing a horse who falls in love with a young human girl and is flayed alive; the girl falls ill, but is cured when the horse descends from heaven and flies her to India.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|10}} The story is told through a dance including sticks with the heads of a horse and human girl on each side.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|10}} This dance is known as the Ebisu Mai, or the "God of wealth dance," and it is said that the dolls become possessed by kami spirits during the performance.<ref name="Fairchild1962" />{{rp|67}}

===Musical performance===
''Itako'' are associated with the {{nihongo|{{ill|Azusa Yumi|en|Azusa Yumi|qid=Q4833134|lt=catalpa bow|1=Azusa Yumi}}|梓弓|azusa yumi}}, a plucked instrument with ties to summoning the dead. The necromantic ritual of plucked instruments is found in the earliest document of Japan's history, the ''[[Kojiki]]'', which describes a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|en|Koto (instrument)|qid=Q289037|lt=koto|1=Koto (instrument)}}'s sound in conjunction with the possession of a medium and the channeling of the dead. Another poem describes the sound of the instrument as a reflection of his wife's spirit possessing the instrument.<ref name="deFerranti">{{cite book|last1=de Ferranti|first1=Hugh|title=The Last Biwa Singer: A Blind Musician in History, Imagination and Performance|date=2009|publisher=East Asia Program, Cornell University|location=Ithaca, NY|isbn=9781942242437|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9781942242437}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}}
</ref>{{rp|35}} The ''[[:en:biwa hoshi|biwa hoshi]]'', itinerant blind priests, have a similar history with the instrument.<ref name="deFerranti" />{{rp|32}}

The chant which opens the ''kamioroshi'' ceremony makes several references to the plucked bow. The chant announces that the first pluck of the string calls down the gods of the village, the second calls down the gods of the prefecture, and the third calls the gods of all prefectures in Japan. Additional notes summon more powerful gods, responsible for ever-increasing spans of land and spiritual realms.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|6}} These gods are sent away in reverse order at the end of the ceremony.<ref name="Naumann" />{{rp|6}}

==Training==
Women usually enter ''itako'' training at a young age, prior to menstruation, at the encouragement of her parents.<ref name="Blacker" /><ref name="Kawamura" /> Before the introduction of special education programs in Japan, this was a choice made by the family to assure that a blind daughter could contribute to the household.<ref name="Blacker" /> Adopting the role of a {{ill|psychic|en|psychic|qid=Q2917466|lt=medium|1=Psychic}} was seen as an acceptable means for blind women to contribute to their local village and household, and avoid becoming a financial burden to their families.<ref name="Kawamura" />

Training for ''itako'' was often paid for with contributions from villagers, rather than the family.<ref name="Kawamura" /> Common aspects of initiation practices for these women were seen among those training in Yamagata, Aomori, and Miyagi prefectures in the 1920s and 1930s. They are trained in various practices, including memorization of [[Shinto]] and {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} prayers and {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}}s. Training typically involved cold-water {{nihongo|purifying baths|水垢離|mizugori}}, which in its most extreme form can involve complete, sustained drenching by ice-cold water for a period of several days.<ref name="Blacker" /> These rituals are observed by the community, which prays for a fast resolution through the early arrival of a marrying deity.<ref name="Kawamura" />

Apprenticeship typically lasts three years, and involves heavy rote memorization and feats of physical [[Ritual purification|purification]].<ref name="Kawamura" /> During this time, the ''itako''-in-training is essentially adopted by a practicing shaman, and performs household work for the family.<ref name="Kawamura" />

===Initiation ceremony===
Ahead of the initiation ceremony, ''itako'' dress in a white {{ill|Kimono|en|Kimono|qid=Q483444|lt=Kimono|1=Kimono}} for several days, similar to a burial gown.<ref name="Kawamura" /> She is not permitted to consume grain, salt, or meat, and must avoid artificial heat for three weeks before the ceremony.<ref>[http://www.nfb.org/Images/nfb/Publications/bm/bm02/bm0205/bm0205dd.htm The Itako—a Spiritual Occupation for Blind Japanese Girls]</ref> The lead-up to the ceremony had been described as incorporating "sleeplessness, semi-starvation and intense cold."<ref name="Blacker1975">{{cite book|last=Blacker|first= Carmen|title=The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan|year=2005|orig-year=1975|publisher=Taylor & Francis|place=Richmond}}</ref>{{rp|122}}

This process usually leads to a loss of consciousness, which is described as the moment in which [[:en:Fudo-Myoo|Fudo Myoo]], or Nittensama, or some other deity, has taken possession of the ''itako'''s body.<ref name="Blacker" /> In some cases, the ''itako'' must collapse while naming the spirit.<ref name="Kawamura">{{cite web|last1=Kawamura|first1=Kunimitsu|title=The Life of a Shamaness: Scenes from the Shamanism of Northeastern Japan|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/cpjr/folkbeliefs/kawamura.html|website=Kokugakuin|access-date=1 February 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/201509190dd355/http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/cpjr/folkbeliefs/kawamura.html|archive-date=19 September 2015}}</ref> In other cases, the names of various deities are written and scattered, while the ''itako'' sweeps over them with a brush until one of them is caught, which denotes the name of the possessing spirit.<ref name="Kawamura" />

At this point, a wedding ceremony, ''kamizukeshiki'',<ref name="UNESCO">{{cite web|title=Itako no Kuchiyose|url=http://www.accu.or.jp/ich/en/arts/A_JPN6.html|website=UNESCO Asian-Pacific Cultural Center|access-date=9 March 2016}}</ref> is performed as an initiation.<ref name="Blacker" /> The ''itako'' trainee is dressed in a red wedding dress, and red rice and fish are consumed to celebrate her marriage to the spirit.<ref name="Blacker" /> It is suggested that the ceremony signals the death of the ''itako'''s life as a burden and her rebirth as a contributing member of the community.<ref name="Kawamura" />

==Contemporary ''itako''==
[[File:Jizo at Osorezan.jpg|thumb|A statue of Jizo at Osorezan]]
In contemporary Japan, ''itako'' are on the decline. In 2009, less than 20 remained, all over the age of 40.<ref name="Fackler-NYTimes">{{cite news |last1=Fackler |first1=Martin |date=21 August 2009 |title=As Japan's Mediums Die, Ancient Tradition Fades |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/08/21/world/asia/21japan.html?_r=0 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20130427121842/http://www.nytimes.com/2009/08/21/world/asia/21japan.html?_r=0 |archive-date=27 April 2013 |access-date=1 February 2016 |work=New York Times }} [http://www.countryakitas.com] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230530180452/https://www.countryakitas.com/ |date=2023-05-30 }}/http://www.nytimes.com/2009/08/21/world/asia/21japan.html Alt URL]</ref> ''Itako'' are increasingly viewed with skepticism and disdain{{why|date=April 2016}}, and contemporary education standards have all but eradicated the need for specialized training for the blind.<ref name="Fackler-NYTimes" />

An annual festival is held on Mt. Osore beside the Entsuji Buddhist temple, which hangs signs disavowing any connection to the ''itako''.<ref name="Schiffer">{{cite journal|last1=Schiffer|first1=Wilhelm|title=Necromancers in the Tohoku|journal=Journal: Contemporary Religions in Japan|date=1967|volume=8|issue=2|url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3186}}</ref>{{rp|179}}<ref name="Dirks" />{{rp|103}} The festival is held July 20–24, coinciding with the {{ill|Obon|en|Obon|qid=Q1154888|lt=Obon|1=Obon}} holiday, which is the traditional day on which spirits of the dead return.<ref name="Schiffer" />{{rp|179}}<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2005|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978dd35797393|page=83}}</ref> They also attend the summer festival at Kawakura Sainokawara.<ref name="Schatt2003" />{{rp|31}} Despite the disavowal of many religious organizations and temples in Japan, both events have become tourist attractions that attract crowds of hundreds. The local government includes the image of an ''itako'' in its tourist brochures, and attempted to fund a permanent ''itako'' position in the nearby temple to encourage sustained tourism throughout the year.<ref name="Schatt2003" />{{rp|31}}

''Itako'', though few in number, have formed an association, the ''itako-ko'' and do occasionally work with Buddhist temples, usually providing support for funerals.<ref name="Schatt2003" />{{rp|30}}
